text: First Families of Toledo Polonia  By Rosemary A. Chorzempa     Before the American Civil War, there were few Polish immigrants in northwest Ohio. In 1866, Poles  from a small area of Poznania west of Znin began to arrive in Toledo, Ohio. Three hundred families have  been documented as immigrants to the Toledo area as of 1877, and no doubt there are more that did not  leave a paper trail.    A database which will be available on the PGSA website includes some or all of the following

on the PGSA website includes some or all of the following  information about each family unit:  names and aliases, parents’ names of adult immigrants,  birth/baptismal, marriage, and death/burial information, origins in Poland (parish and/or village names),  ships’ names and arrival dates, some information about children, residence (parish names, street names  and neighborhoods in Toledo or other cities), occupations, known migrations from Toledo to other cities  in the U.S., and known remarriages.

Toledo to other cities  in the U.S., and known remarriages.  A Brief History of Poland for Genealogists     There were many reasons for Polish emigration. Studying Polish and European history and geography  can shed a lot of light on why your ancestors settled where and when they did.      By 1800, Poland no longer existed as a separate state, its vast area being divided up during three  separate partitions by Russia, Prussia (Germany) and Austria. Poland did not exist as a separate nation for

and Austria. Poland did not exist as a separate nation for  over 120 years, until after World War I. While checking various documents, you may find your ancestors  giving these three countries, or Russian-Poland, Prussian-Poland, German-Poland, Austrian-Poland or  Galicia, as their origins. At times they may have also stated “Poland” as their birthplace.     Poland was the breadbasket of Europe until about 1600 A.D.  Polish wheat was of the highest quality in

about 1600 A.D.  Polish wheat was of the highest quality in  Europe. Poland exported many goods and crops to the rest of the continent. The economy was very good,  and even the peasant quality of life was better than it was in the next couple centuries. Then the climate  cooled, called “the Little Ice Age” in Europe, lasting more than 200 years, until the mid-1800s. Droughts  became more common. Most people’s diets suffered from the wars and political and economic changes,

suffered from the wars and political and economic changes,  even through the 1800s. By the 1850s, 80% of the population lived from agriculture. In the Prussian zone,  where most of the earliest Polish immigrants came from, 55% of the residents relied on agriculture. The  population increased rapidly through the 1800s, even with mass emigration.     The potato was introduced into the Polish diet in the early 1800s. It became the staple food of the

diet in the early 1800s. It became the staple food of the  growing number of town dwellers by the 1840s. Poland and the rest of Europe was hit by the potato pest  in the 1846-1855 time period, causing disease, starvation, an increase in the mortality rate and decrease in  population.      Asiatic cholera epidemics swept Poland and Europe in 1831, 1847-48, 1854, 1866, 1873, 1884 and  1892. Uprisings and revolutions by the Poles against the occupying Prussians, Russians and Austrians

Poles against the occupying Prussians, Russians and Austrians  occurred in 1830-31, 1844-48 and 1863.      Beginning in 1864, the Russification programs against the Poles intensified as a result of the January  (1863) Uprising. Evidence of this can still be seen today when searching Catholic church records from the  Russian-occupied areas. Instead of Latin, church records were required to be written in the Russian  language.

records were required to be written in the Russian  language.     Beginning in 1867, Prime Minister Otto von Bismarck began his Kulturkampf. By 1871, his anti- Catholic, anti-Polish plan was being implemented. More intense pressure was applied to Germanize the  Poles. Land was taken away from Polish farmers and sold to German immigrant farmers brought into the  Polish lands by Bismarck. Many Catholic churches, monasteries and convents were closed, and some

churches, monasteries and convents were closed, and some  were converted to Evangelical (Lutheran) churches. Check LDS microfilms of records from your Polish  parish for a parish history, or try online, as many parishes and towns in Poland now publish detailed  online histories. The Slownik Geograficzny also contains parish histories in the town entries.     The Austrian-occupied area, called Malopolska or Little Poland, was named Galicia by the Austrian

Malopolska or Little Poland, was named Galicia by the Austrian  government. (Wielkopolska, or Great Poland, was in the Prussian-occupied area.) Little Poland was the  most underdeveloped area of the country with most of the population engaged in agriculture. This area  produced the greatest number of emigrants.     Many Poles were conscripted into the occupying forces’ armies, and forced to fight in far-away wars.

forces’ armies, and forced to fight in far-away wars.  Conscription notations can be seen in many Catholic Church baptismal record books next to the male  children’s birth records. These notations were written in German script in the margins, probably by the  draft board, and added many years after the births occurred when the young men were of draft age.       A combination of all the above events caused the mass emigration of Poles. Polish emigrants not only

caused the mass emigration of Poles. Polish emigrants not only  came to the United States, but also to Canada, South America, France, England and Australia. There were  about 110,000 emigrants (total) who had left Poland by 1870. By 1914, there were about 4.2 million.  Polish Immigration to Toledo     The earliest identified Polish immigrants to Toledo were Peter Szynka (aka Schinkey, Shinke) born  about 1826 and his first wife Catherine Wisniewska b about 1830. Peter Schenk 28 a watchmaker and his

Wisniewska b about 1830. Peter Schenk 28 a watchmaker and his  wife Catherina 24 sailed on the SS Robert Peel from London arriving in New York on 22 November  1854. Their only known child was Mary who was baptized in St. Joseph Church in Maumee, Ohio in  November 1858; she married and had children. They were farmers and lived in Waynesfield Twp. in  1870. By 1900, they lived in Maumee village on Sophia Street.

1870. By 1900, they lived in Maumee village on Sophia Street.      According to “Polacy w Toledo,” written in 1908 by Stefan Nesterowicz, Peter Szynka was from the  village and parish of Tuchola near Naklo nad Notec (actually quite a few miles north of Naklo). Peter was  the brother of Anna Szynka who married Jan Kortas.  The Kortas family was also from Tuchola, and the  families were related. The Kalas family may also have been from Tuchola, and was also related to the

may also have been from Tuchola, and was also related to the  Kortases. The Kortas and Kalas families immigrated in 1866 and 1868.     Catherine Szynka/Shinkey died in 1894 and was buried in St. Joseph Cemetery, Maumee. Peter married  another Catherine later that same year at St. Hedwig Church. His second wife was the widow of John  Kledzik, her father was Maciej Chlopek. The second Mrs. Shinkey died in 1927, in Kuhschwantz. Peter

The second Mrs. Shinkey died in 1927, in Kuhschwantz. Peter  died 9 February 1918 in Monclova Twp, Ohio and is also buried in St. Joseph Cemetery.     While there were a few individuals and their families who immigrated from Poland and lived in Toledo  before 1870, they were mostly Jewish or German-Lutherans. A few Polish Catholics did arrive in Toledo  in the late 1860s, but the mass immigration of Poles to Toledo finally commenced about the year 1870.

of Poles to Toledo finally commenced about the year 1870.  According to author Stefan Nesterowicz, there were about 100 Polish families in Toledo in 1874. In 1880,  there were 150 Polish families in the seventh ward (Lagrinka) and 80 families in the eighth ward  (Kuhschwanz).      A small number of Polish immigrants served in Ohio regiments during the American Civil War. Some  of these from northwest Ohio are noted in this First Families database.

from northwest Ohio are noted in this First Families database.     Some of these families who first settled in Toledo early on, moved to Manistee, Michigan and the  Radom, Illinois area in the mid-1870s due to the poor economy in Toledo. A “clan” of related families  originating in Lopienno parish, Poland moved to Radom (in the Belleville diocese of Illinois) and its  surrounding farm communities. Those identified so far:  Anielak, Borowiak, Ciesliewicz, Czarnecki,

identified so far:  Anielak, Borowiak, Ciesliewicz, Czarnecki,  Czerniejewski, Grzegorzewski, Kalas, Koczorowski, Kujawa, Lawecki, Martek, Musielak, Przybylski,  Stoinski, Stelmaszewski, Szubarga (and Janowski), Wesolowski. Poles who lived in Toledo also had  relatives in Fremont, Ohio (St. Joseph), Marblehead, Ohio, and South Bend, Indiana. There were small  enclaves of Poles in St. Boniface parish in Oak Harbor, Ohio (Ottawa County), Immaculate Conception

in Oak Harbor, Ohio (Ottawa County), Immaculate Conception  parish in Port Clinton, Ohio (Ottawa County) and St. Mary parish in Sandusky, Ohio (Erie County).     As more and more Poles arrived in America, they wrote home to their families and friends back in the  Old Country. Many adult siblings, and even families of three generations at a time, came to Toledo in the  1870s and 1880s.      Some entries in the Slownik Geograficzny mention that the population of the entire village left for

mention that the population of the entire village left for  America. (Slownik Geograficzny = Geographical Dictionary of  Poland, a very useful reference available  on a CD-rom from the PGSA, describing every village in the Polish territories, written in 1880-1900, the  time of our ancestors’ emigration). Folwark Ameryka was the name of a large manor and farm near  Szubin, an area from which many of the first Toledo Polonia came.

an area from which many of the first Toledo Polonia came.     They came from Poznania (Posen in German), specifically from the area bounded by Kamien Krajenski  (just north of Bydgoszcz) on the north to Poznan on the south, and on the west Rosko (northwest of  Poznan) to Inowroclaw on the east. Most of these pioneers came from the Szubin-Znin-Lopienno-Kcynia  region, an area roughly 15 miles by 18 miles.     The majority of them came from the parish towns of Znin, Szubin, Kcynia, Lopienno, Cerekwica,

the parish towns of Znin, Szubin, Kcynia, Lopienno, Cerekwica,  Dziewierzewo, Golancz, Czeszewo Wagrowiec, Sadki, Smogulec, Gorzyce, Labiszyn, Rogozno,  Brzyskorzystew, Juncewo, Swiatkowo, Czewujewo, Rogowo, Trzemeszno, Poznan, Miescisko, Lekno,  Rosko, Srebnagora, Slupy, Ryszewko, Lechlin, Ostrowite Prymasowskie, Popowo Koscielne, Podlesie  Koscielne, Szaradowo, Witkowo, Czerniejewo, Kostrzyn, Gluchowo, Kwieciszewo, Inowroclaw, Bysla,

Kostrzyn, Gluchowo, Kwieciszewo, Inowroclaw, Bysla,  Makowarsko, Blugowo, Glesno, and Gniezno.  (See map...Ancestral towns & villages marked in red.)     Many villages in Poland were inhabited by as few as a dozen people to a hundred or two, and were too  small to support their own parish and church. Churches were built in larger villages and in towns, and one  parish may have served a dozen small villages surrounding it. Thus, the names of the villages and towns

surrounding it. Thus, the names of the villages and towns  above are the location of the parish churches, and not necessarily the name of the immigrant’s village.  When the immigrant was giving the pastor in America his information for his marriage record, for  example, he was asked where he was baptized.  These parish towns’ names were taken from our local  Catholic church records and parish records from Poland.

Catholic church records and parish records from Poland.      The oldest Polish settlement in America at Panna Maria, Texas was begun in 1854. The Polish origins  of these Texas immigrants is nearly identical to that of Toledo. Texas Poles came from the parishes of  Margonin, Jaktorowo, Smogulec, Kcynia, Panigrodz, Czeszewo, Srebnagora, Dziewierzewo, Slupy,  Szaradowo, Szubin, Skoki and Lechlin.   The Early Polish Settlements in Toledo

Skoki and Lechlin.   The Early Polish Settlements in Toledo     Before the 1875 founding of St. Hedwig, the first Polish Roman Catholic Church in Toledo, the Polish  Catholics were served by the two German Catholic Churches in Toledo, St. Mary and Saints Peter and  Paul. These Poles were familiar with the German language, as that was the official language of their  homeland. These two German churches also happened to be near the areas of the Polish settlements in

happened to be near the areas of the Polish settlements in  Toledo, one to the north of downtown (Lagrinka) and one to the west of downtown (Kuhschwanz).     Vistula (the Latin word for the Polish river Wisla) was a town platted out in 1833 and located in the Old  North End, which merged with the rival town of Port Lawrence (immediately to the west of Vistula, the  site of the current downtown Toledo) founded in 1817. Toledo arose from this merger in 1837.

founded in 1817. Toledo arose from this merger in 1837.     The first Poles in Toledo settled in the older southern portion of what is now known as “Lagrinka,” the  Polish settlement in north Toledo, beginning in 1867. This area was first known as “the Polish  Settlement” in the 1870s and 1880s. These first homes were located well to the south of the site of the  first (and current) St. Hedwig Church, in the area of Lagrange to Cherry streets, and Bancroft to Frederick

area of Lagrange to Cherry streets, and Bancroft to Frederick  streets. The largest cluster of Poles lived in and around the 900 block of Frederick Street. After St.  Hedwig Church was built in 1875, the Poles settled in the area surrounding the first church, which was  built on the north side of the 100 block of Dexter Street, near Warsaw Street (then called Locust Street).  After the original church building was torn down, the property became Dexter Park, a city park, and its

down, the property became Dexter Park, a city park, and its  location is marked with a historical marker.     By 1880, there were two distinct “Lagrinkas.” One, to the south of Frederick Street, and the newer one  to the north of Hausman Street. The newer Polish Settlement surrounded St. Hedwig Church; most Poles  lived in the 100 blocks of Mettler Street (then called Franklin), Everett and Dexter Streets, and Bronson

called Franklin), Everett and Dexter Streets, and Bronson  Avenue. Two large tracts of land, privately owned, divided the two Lagrinkas, but were soon sold and  eventually Poles and other residents moved into that area.     The Poles in north Toledo walked over a mile south down Cherry Street to St. Mary Church, which at  that time was located on the west side of Cherry Street at Michigan, on the southern edge of the Wabash

Cherry Street at Michigan, on the southern edge of the Wabash  & Erie Canal bed (aka “the big ditch.” In Lucas Co. records, many people resided and/or died at the big  ditch.) St. Mary was founded as a German Catholic Church in 1854. St. Mary Cemetery was located at the  northwest corner of Lagrange Street and Manhattan Blvd. As was the custom and the law, the cemetery  was outside the city limits (until 1868 when the city annexed the area). St. Francis de Sales Church,

when the city annexed the area). St. Francis de Sales Church,  organized in 1841 and located just three blocks to the south of St. Mary Church, also had its cemetery in  the same location. Eventually the two side-by-side cemeteries merged into Mount Carmel Cemetery. One  can still find gravestones that date from the 1840s.      No evidence of Polish baptisms, marriages or funerals are found in the early St. Francis de Sales records

funerals are found in the early St. Francis de Sales records  (at that time an Irish parish), nor those of St. Joseph Parish (then a French parish, organized in 1854),  located about three blocks to the east of St. Francis and St. Mary parishes, at Locust and Erie Streets.     Places of employment for the earliest Polish settlers included the Milburn Wagon Works (Monroe &  Detroit), Osborn, Chase and Swayne wood moulding company (Chestnut & Champlain), B. Meilink &

wood moulding company (Chestnut & Champlain), B. Meilink &  Co. furniture (Summit & Cherry), Hubbard, Newhouse & Cable flooring (Lagrange & Water), Christian  Guenther dyer and scourer (47 Adams Street), J. Brereton carpentry, William Peter carpentry, Finlay &  Zahn, Buckeye Brewery (Bush & Champlain), Toledo Gas Light and Coke Co. (69 Madison), Lake Shore  and Michigan Southern Railroad.    By 1923, Poles were the largest ethnic group in the city of Toledo, generating the need for more parishes

in the city of Toledo, generating the need for more parishes  to serve them. Currently, there are about 60,000 residents of the Greater Toledo area who claim Polish  ancestry and heritage.  Kuhschwanz Settlement     In 1871, Poles began to settle in the area now known as the Nebraska Avenue area or “Kuhschwanz,”  “Cow’s Tail” in German. It probably was named by the German immigrants living in this area of town

was named by the German immigrants living in this area of town  known as Lenk’s Hill, a German settlement. It really was the “Cow’s Tail,” or as some might say “out in  the sticks,” as this Polish settlement was literally on the edge of the Toledo city limits. At that time, the  spot where St. Anthony Church stands now was outside the Toledo city limits, in Adams Township. Some  of these people farmed in the area.

in Adams Township. Some  of these people farmed in the area.      The earliest Polish residents of Kuhschwanz lived in the area roughly bounded by Belmont, Detroit,  Campbell and Hawley. This area was just to the west of the German Lenk’s Hill settlement, on the  western edge of the city of Toledo and beyond. The Kuhschwanz Poles had to walk about a mile to the  east, crossing Swan Creek and the Canal bed, to attend church services at SS. Peter & Paul Church. Many

to attend church services at SS. Peter & Paul Church. Many  of the residents of this area were buried in St. Patrick’s Cemetery, according to Lucas County death  records, though there is no record of such a cemetery ever being in existence, according to the Toledo  Diocese. St. Patrick Church was (and still is in the same location) in Lenk’s Hill. It was founded in 1862  as an Irish parish.     SS. Peter & Paul Church was founded in 1866 as a German parish and located on St. Clair Street near

1866 as a German parish and located on St. Clair Street near  Harrison, near the canal locks and south of Swan Creek. There are not many records of Poles in this  parish until early 1873, when there were 15 baptisms of Polish infants that year. The Saints Peter & Paul  Cemetery was at the intersection of Western and Wayne Streets, and served from 1867 to 1923. In the  1920s the bodies were reinterred at Calvary Cemetery. The old Foodtown store at Western and Airport  sits on this former cemetery property.

at Western and Airport  sits on this former cemetery property.  The First Polish Roman Catholic Church in Toledo – St. Hedwig (October 16, 1875)     After a sufficient number of Polish families immigrated to Toledo, they sought to form a Polish Roman  Catholic parish. The residents of both Polish settlements received permission from the Bishop of the  Cleveland Diocese. (The diocese of Toledo was not formed until 1910.)

Diocese. (The diocese of Toledo was not formed until 1910.)     October 16, 1875 marked the official beginning of St. Hedwig Parish with the celebration of the first  Mass in the basement of St. Mary Church. Construction was begun shortly after, and the red brick church  was built at 100 S. Dexter Street, between Locust (now Warsaw) and Lagrange Streets. The church was  blessed on May 7, 1876. Father Vincent Lewandowski was the first pastor

May 7, 1876. Father Vincent Lewandowski was the first pastor  Once St. Hedwig was established, almost all of the Polish families went to St. Hedwig for the sacraments.  Records of Polish sacraments at St. Mary and SS. Peter & Paul parishes became almost non-existent after  1875.     There was a fire in St. Hedwig Church in 1886, during which time the first book of death records  disappeared. That is part of the reason for including Lucas County death records in this project, as well as

Lucas County death records in this project, as well as  to include Poles who lived in Kuhschwanz or other parts of the city. After the fire, the second and final  building was built in 1891, located at 1916 Lagrange Street, only one block away from the original St.  Hedwig Church.     In 2005, St. Adalbert was twinned with St. Hedwig and shared a pastor.     On 1 July 2010, St. Adalbert and St. Hedwig parishes were “suppressed,” a diocesan term that means

Hedwig parishes were “suppressed,” a diocesan term that means  each parish was terminated with all metrical records from both parishes sent to the diocese. All societies  and employees of the parishes (Altar Society, choir, office staff, etc.) were terminated. Pastor Fr. Richard  Philiposki, of the Society of Christ (an order based in Poland) was reassigned by his order to another city.  New records were to be begun; new staff hired; and Fr. Joseph Poggemeyer was named the pastor of the

hired; and Fr. Joseph Poggemeyer was named the pastor of the  newly-created parish called Sts. Adalbert and Hedwig Parish. Fr. Poggemeyer also remained pastor of his  former parish of St. Joseph (the once-French parish founded in 1854). An announcement was made that  the St. Hedwig Church building was to be open only for weddings and special occasions; and that it will  no longer hold Sunday or daily Masses.

and that it will  no longer hold Sunday or daily Masses.     In 2012, St. Hedwig Church building was permanently closed by the diocese, its assets (stained glass  windows, altars, statues, pews, etc.) sold. It now remains an empty shell.  Additional Polish Parishes in Toledo     Several other ethnic Polish Roman Catholic parishes were formed in Toledo after St. Hedwig..  St. Anthony Parish:  founded in 1882, located at 1416 Nebraska Ave. Kuhschwanz neighborhood. This

located at 1416 Nebraska Ave. Kuhschwanz neighborhood. This  was the first Polish parish formed after St. Hedwig. This parish was also founded by Father Vincent  Lewandowski, the founder of St. Hedwig. He served as the pastor of both parishes for several years until  June 1885.  St. Anthony was closed 30 June 2005.  St. Adalbert Parish:  founded in 1907 by the division of St. Hedwig’s area along Park St., north of Park

division of St. Hedwig’s area along Park St., north of Park  St. went to St. Adalbert, south of Park St. remained in St. Hedwig. Located at 3233 Lagrange in the St.  Lagrinka neighborhood. Currently the parish is called St. Adalbert-St. Hedwig.  St. Stanislaus Kostka Parish (usually referred to as St. Stanislaus):  founded in 1909 by the division of  St. Anthony’s area along Detroit Ave. East of Detroit remained in St. Anthony. West of Detroit was St.

of Detroit remained in St. Anthony. West of Detroit was St.  Stanislaus. Located at 415 N. Detroit Ave. Kuhschwanz neighborhood. Closed 30 June 2005.  Nativity Parish (officially Church of the Nativity):  Organized as a Roman Catholic Parish in 1922,  originally a Polish National Catholic Church. It ultimately merged with St. Anthony. Located at 1124  Nebraska Ave. Kuhschwanz neighborhood. Closed in 1982.  St. Vincent de Paul Parish:  founded in 1924, located at 1035 Woodward Ave. Lagrinka neighborhood.

in 1924, located at 1035 Woodward Ave. Lagrinka neighborhood.  Closed 30 June 2005.  St. Hyacinth Parish:  founded in 1928, located at 719 Evesham Ave. (Parkside Blvd. and Victory St.)  Kuhschwanz neighborhood. Twinned with St. Charles Boromeo Parish.     Though not officially Polish ethnic parishes, St. Mary Magdalene Parish in Rossford, Ohio (founded  1903) and Our Lady of Lourdes Parish on Hill Avenue in Holland, Ohio (founded 1927) had many Polish  members.

in Holland, Ohio (founded 1927) had many Polish  members.      Many Poles settled early on in Adams Township (just west of St. Anthony parish and west past the  University of Toledo campus), Swanton, Ohio and Holland, Ohio, Bedford Township in Monroe County,  Michigan, and near Oak Harbor, Ohio (belonging to St. Boniface Parish).  SOURCES AND RESOURCES     The following publications are very useful to people researching their Polish families’ ancestry. Some

to people researching their Polish families’ ancestry. Some  of these have been used as sources of information for this project.  St. Hedwig Parish records  St. Mary Parish records  SS. Peter & Paul Parish records  St. Boniface, Oak Harbor Parish records  Lucas County death records  U.S. Federal Census records  Toledo City Directories  U.S. Immigration and Passenger Lists  LDS microfilms of Roman Catholic parish records in Poland

LDS microfilms of Roman Catholic parish records in Poland  Poznan Project (http://bindweed.man.poznan.pl/posen/project.php) An index of marriages in the Poznan  district, from about 1820 to 1889, work in progress.  Toledo-Lucas County Public Library Local History & Genealogy Department, maps (Toledo Additions  Map, 1837-1963, by the Toledo-Lucas County Plan Commission  Korzenie Polskie – Polish Roots by Rosemary A. Chorzempa, Genealogical Publishing Co., Inc.,

by Rosemary A. Chorzempa, Genealogical Publishing Co., Inc.,  Baltimore, Maryland, 1993 (still in print), a book on how to research your Polish ancestors  The First Hundred Years, 1875-1975, A History of St. Hedwig Parish, Toledo, Ohio by the St. Hedwig  Parish Centennial Anniversary Committee (David G. Chelminski, Larry Chorzempa, chairman Rosemary  A. Chorzempa, Mark D. Dembinski, Gerald A. Mazuchowski, Michael J. Niemiec and Melvin R.  Ogrodowski), 1975.

Michael J. Niemiec and Melvin R.  Ogrodowski), 1975.  Polska Atlas Drogowy (Road Atlas of Poland), GeoCenter, Warszawa, Poland, 1996.  The Latin Church in the Polish Commonwealth in 1772, a Map and Index of Localities by Stanislaw  Litak, Polish Genealogical Society of America, Chicago, Illinois, 1990. (Polish Roman Catholic parishes  maps, use it to find out what parish your ancestor belonged to, then look at LDS records. This book shows

belonged to, then look at LDS records. This book shows  parishes in the old Commonwealth, which extended far to the east and south of current Poland.)  Roman Catholic Parishes in the Polish People’s Republic in 1984 by Lidia Mullerowa, Polish  Genealogical Society of America, Chicago, Illinois, 1995. (Maps of parish locations in current Poland.)  Slownik Geograficzny Krolestwa Polskiego (Geographical Dictionary of the Kingdom of Poland and

(Geographical Dictionary of the Kingdom of Poland and  other slavonic countries) digital edition on CD-rom, Rafal T. Prinke, Polish Genealogical Society of  America, 2003. (The original paper volumes were written and printed c. 1880 to 1900, and show  information about towns and small villages when your ancestors were living there. Very useful for  information and locating villages and to which parish they belonged.)

and locating villages and to which parish they belonged.)  Germans to America, Lists of Passengers Arriving at U.S. Ports by Ira A. Glazier and William P. Filby,  Scholarly Resources Inc., Wilmington, Deleware, 1988-    . (Indexes of passengers arriving in the U.S.  onboard German ships, from German ports, coming from German areas in Europe or having German  names.)  Toledo’s nineteenth-century Polonia:  Ziomki I Rodacy (Country Folk and Compatriots) by David  Chelminski, 1989. (Written in English)

Compatriots) by David  Chelminski, 1989. (Written in English)  The Ethnicity of the Poles in Toledo c. 1830-1886 by David Chelminski, 1978. (Both of Dr. Chelminski’s  publications are available at the Toledo-Lucas County Public Library.)  Polacy w Toledo [Poles in Toledo] by Stefan Nesterowicz, Anthony Paryski Publishing Co., Toledo,  Ohio, 1908. [written in Polish language, with photographs of old pioneers and businesses and homes]

with photographs of old pioneers and businesses and homes]  Peter Ueberroth, Archivist for the Roman Catholic Diocese of Toledo, who provided information for  some of the old churches and cemeteries.  St. Michael Parish records from Radom, Illinois (and other parishes in the Diocese of Belleville, Illinois),  and all Diocese of Toledo parish records are available at https://www.familysearch.org.

Northbridge Biologics
Internal Operations Memorandum Document ID: NBB-OPS-INT-042
Classification: Internal Use Only
Prepared by: Strategic Operations Office
Last Updated: February 12, 2026
Distribution: Operations, Product, Regulatory, IT Support, Customer Success Subject: Q2 Readiness Plan for Project Harbor and Associated Workflow Changes Overview This memo provides a consolidated update on Project Harbor, the internal codename for Northbridge Biologics’ next-phase customer delivery framework.

Northbridge Biologics’ next-phase customer delivery framework. The purpose of Project Harbor is to reduce turnaround time for custom assay onboarding, improve internal visibility across fulfillment stages, and create a more consistent support experience for enterprise customers. This document summarizes the current operating assumptions, implementation milestones, cross-functional responsibilities, known risks, and communication expectations for the Q2 transition period.

and communication expectations for the Q2 transition period. Project Harbor was initiated in response to three recurring operational issues identified during the Q4 and Q1 reviews. First, onboarding requests were being routed through multiple teams without a single ownership model, which caused delays and duplicate work. Second, customer-facing timelines were often based on informal estimates rather than standardized capacity rules.

on informal estimates rather than standardized capacity rules. Third, issue escalation from Customer Success to Product and Regulatory frequently lacked the minimum context required for rapid review. The Harbor initiative is intended to address these gaps through process simplification, structured handoff templates, and limited automation in internal ticketing. Scope Project Harbor applies to all new enterprise assay onboarding requests submitted on or after April 1, 2026.

assay onboarding requests submitted on or after April 1, 2026. Existing customer workflows will continue under the current model unless specifically migrated by Operations leadership. The new framework applies to the following departments: Commercial Intake, Product Operations, Regulatory Documentation, Laboratory Coordination, Data Systems, and Customer Success. Finance and Legal are not direct owners of the Harbor workflow, but may be consulted for contract-dependent exceptions.

but may be consulted for contract-dependent exceptions. The Harbor framework does not replace quality management procedures, change control requirements, or customer contract review. It is an operating layer intended to improve coordination before a project enters formal validation or regulated execution. Teams should not interpret Harbor status updates as approvals for release, compliance signoff, or production readiness. Core Workflow Changes

signoff, or production readiness. Core Workflow Changes Beginning in Q2, all onboarding requests must enter through the Intake Gateway form in ServiceHub rather than through email, chat, or spreadsheet tracking. Requests submitted outside ServiceHub should be redirected rather than manually processed. The Intake Gateway form requires five mandatory fields: customer name, request category, target launch date, region, and requested assay panel.

target launch date, region, and requested assay panel. Optional fields may be completed later, but the mandatory fields must be present for a submission to receive a Harbor tracking ID. Once submitted, each request will be automatically tagged using a two-level priority system. Priority level is determined by commercial commitment and implementation urgency. Tier 1 requests include signed enterprise expansions, regulated customer migrations, and executive-approved pilots.

regulated customer migrations, and executive-approved pilots. Tier 2 requests include exploratory opportunities, unsupported configuration requests, and general capability inquiries. Teams should note that Tier 1 designation accelerates review order but does not waive documentation requirements.

review order but does not waive documentation requirements. After intake, the request will move through four standard checkpoints: triage, feasibility review, documentation review, and launch preparation. Each checkpoint has a designated owner. Triage is owned by Commercial Intake, feasibility review by Product Operations, documentation review by Regulatory Documentation, and launch preparation by Laboratory Coordination with support from Customer Success. A checkpoint may not be marked complete unless the required notes are entered in the Harbor activity log.
the required notes are entered in the Harbor activity log. System and Data Notes

are entered in the Harbor activity log. System and Data Notes The Harbor dashboard will be available through the internal Portal beginning March 25, 2026. Access will initially be limited to managers, workflow owners, and assigned coordinators. General employee access will be expanded in a later phase after role-based filtering is validated. The dashboard is intended as the source of truth for request status, owner assignment, and expected completion dates. Teams should discontinue parallel status tracking in personal spreadsheets once Harbor is live.

status tracking in personal spreadsheets once Harbor is live. Data entered into Harbor should not include protected health information, raw customer datasets, or attachment files containing unredacted identifiers. Harbor is not a document repository for regulated source records. Supporting files should continue to be stored in the approved ShareVault structure with the Harbor tracking ID referenced in the filename. IT Support will audit storage practices during the first six weeks of rollout to identify noncompliant usage.

the first six weeks of rollout to identify noncompliant usage. The current integration between Harbor and ServiceHub is one-way only. Updates entered in Harbor will not write back to historical tickets in ServiceHub. For this reason, coordinators must verify that customer-facing updates in CRM match the latest Harbor status before sending timeline commitments. A bidirectional sync is planned for a later release but has not yet passed internal validation. Roles and Responsibilities

not yet passed internal validation. Roles and Responsibilities Department managers are responsible for ensuring that at least one primary and one backup owner are assigned for each Harbor checkpoint. Backup owners must be trained before March 28. Individual contributors are expected to update notes within one business day of completing a review step. Delayed note entry creates downstream reporting errors and reduces forecast accuracy, which was a major issue in the previous operating model.

which was a major issue in the previous operating model. Customer Success should not promise a launch date based only on verbal confirmation from a single department. A launch estimate may only be communicated after the request reaches documented feasibility review and receives a preliminary timing band in Harbor. Timing bands are defined as Standard, Accelerated, or Complex. These are internal planning indicators and should not be copied verbatim into customer messaging without approved wording.

verbatim into customer messaging without approved wording. Product Operations will maintain the list of supported assay configurations. Requests outside the supported matrix should be labeled Exception Review Required. Exception reviews are discussed during the Tuesday cross-functional operations meeting and require representation from Product, Regulatory, and Laboratory Coordination. If a request is not suitable for exception review, the decision and rationale must still be recorded in Harbor for reporting and future analysis.
still be recorded in Harbor for reporting and future analysis. Known Risks

in Harbor for reporting and future analysis. Known Risks The largest near-term risk is partial adoption. If teams continue to manage requests through side channels, the dashboard will become incomplete and leadership reporting will be unreliable. The second major risk is overuse of Tier 1 classification. Managers should avoid inflating priority labels to bypass queue discipline, as this will reduce the credibility of the system and create conflict between departments. The third risk is inconsistent documentation quality, especially in feasibility review notes.

documentation quality, especially in feasibility review notes. Another operational concern is training variance across regions. North America and EU teams have already completed the pilot workflow walkthrough, but APAC onboarding leads will not complete training until late March. During the first two weeks of April, APAC requests may experience longer triage times while support coverage stabilizes. This should be reflected in internal planning but not framed as a system failure. Communication Guidance

but not framed as a system failure. Communication Guidance Questions about process ownership should be directed to the Strategic Operations Office, while technical access issues should be routed to IT Support under the “Harbor Access” category. Employees should avoid creating informal FAQs in chat channels, since outdated answers tend to persist and create confusion. The official reference materials will be maintained in the Portal knowledge page under Operations > Workflow Standards > Project Harbor.

page under Operations > Workflow Standards > Project Harbor. Managers are encouraged to collect examples of ambiguous cases during the first month of rollout. These examples will be used to refine the training guide and improve decision rules for future automation. Feedback should be specific, documented, and tied to a Harbor tracking ID whenever possible. Immediate Action Items

a Harbor tracking ID whenever possible. Immediate Action Items 1. All department leads must confirm checkpoint owners and backups by March 22.
2. IT Support must validate access groups and manager permissions by March 25.
3. Customer Success leadership must retire legacy intake spreadsheet usage by March 31.
4. Product Operations must publish the current supported assay matrix to the Portal before go-live.
5. Regional managers must confirm training completion status during the March 27 operations call.

training completion status during the March 27 operations call. Closing Note Project Harbor is not intended to add procedural overhead. Its purpose is to reduce ambiguity, improve handoffs, and make request status visible across teams without requiring repeated manual follow-up. The long-term success of Harbor will depend less on the software itself and more on consistent usage, disciplined documentation, and clear ownership at every checkpoint.

documentation, and clear ownership at every checkpoint. Departments are expected to treat Q2 as a structured rollout period, with measured adjustments based on observed bottlenecks rather than ad hoc workarounds.

The Cedar Harbor Pilot is a small internal evaluation project created to test whether a local retrieval-augmented assistant can run reliably on older mobile hardware without using any cloud APIs.  The project is managed by Neel Varma under the internal identifier CHP-3182 and officially began on 2025-10-14 at the West Annex Lab, Room C7.  Its main goal is to compare lightweight local models for document search and grounded question answering using a limited offline document set.

question answering using a limited offline document set.  At the moment, the test collection contains 11 notes, 4 short reference guides, and 2 archived reports. The preferred answer model for the current phase is qwen3:0.6b, while the embedding model used for retrieval is embeddinggemma. The internal codename for the project is Silent Orbit, the budget ceiling is 2400 USD, and the scheduled review date is 2025-12-05. All indexed files are stored under /data/projects/cedar_harbor/, and the verification phrase recorded for internal testing is "amber circuit".

phrase recorded for internal testing is "amber circuit".  The project is strictly experimental, not cleared for production deployment, and no external API calls are permitted.  The final summary document is supposed to be delivered to Leena Das after the evaluation is complete.  No device serial numbers, email addresses, Wi-Fi credentials, or hardware chip details are included in this note.

NEXAGEN BIOSCIENCES, INC.
Internal Policy & Procedures Manual
Document ID: NXG-POL-MASTER-2024
Version: 4.2.1
Effective Date: March 1, 2024
Supersedes: Version 4.1.9 (January 15, 2024)
Classification: INTERNAL USE ONLY
Document Owner: Office of Compliance and Quality Assurance
Review Cycle: Semi-annual
Next Review: September 1, 2024 SECTION 1: HUMAN RESOURCES POLICIES POL-HR-001: Employee Classification and Compensation

POLICIES POL-HR-001: Employee Classification and Compensation NexaGen Biosciences classifies all personnel into four tiers: Tier I (Research Associates and Lab Technicians), Tier II (Senior Scientists and Bioinformatics Analysts), Tier III (Principal Investigators and Department Leads), and Tier IV (Directors, VPs, and C-suite). Compensation adjustments occur annually during the Performance Review Cycle (PRC) initiated each October. Effective January 2024, base salary bands are as follows:

Effective January 2024, base salary bands are as follows: Tier I: $52,000 – $74,000 annually Tier II: $78,000 – $112,000 annually Tier III: $118,000 – $165,000 annually Tier IV: $170,000 and above, subject to Board approval.

Tier IV: $170,000 and above, subject to Board approval. Overtime eligibility applies exclusively to Tier I employees classified as non-exempt under FLSA guidelines. Tier II and above are classified as exempt. Remote work stipends of $75/month are available to employees whose primary work location is remote for more than 15 business days per calendar month, pending approval via Form NXG-HR-Remote-07. POL-HR-002: Paid Time Off and Leave Entitlements PTO accrues at the following rates:

Off and Leave Entitlements PTO accrues at the following rates: Tier I: 15 days/year (1.25 days/month)
Tier II: 18 days/year (1.5 days/month)
Tier III: 22 days/year
Tier IV: Discretionary, minimum 20 days/year PTO does not roll over beyond 10 days at fiscal year-end (December 31). Any unused PTO above the 10-day cap is forfeited and not compensable. This policy was revised in Version 4.1.9; prior versions allowed rollover up to 15 days.

Version 4.1.9; prior versions allowed rollover up to 15 days. Parental leave is governed separately under POL-HR-007, which provides 16 weeks of fully paid leave for primary caregivers and 6 weeks for secondary caregivers, regardless of gender or Tier classification. Adoption events qualify on the same terms as biological birth events.

events qualify on the same terms as biological birth events. Bereavement leave is 5 business days for immediate family (spouse, child, parent, sibling) and 3 business days for extended family. "Extended family" is defined as grandparents, grandchildren, in-laws, and domestic partners not otherwise covered as immediate family. POL-HR-004: Performance Improvement Plans and Disciplinary Action

Performance Improvement Plans and Disciplinary Action Performance concerns are addressed through a structured Progressive Discipline Framework (PDF) consisting of three stages: Stage 1 – Verbal Counseling: Documented by the direct supervisor within 5 business days of the triggering event. No formal HR involvement required at this stage.

event. No formal HR involvement required at this stage. Stage 2 – Written Warning: Issued by HR in coordination with the supervisor. Triggers a 60-day Performance Improvement Plan (PIP). The PIP must specify at least three measurable improvement criteria with evaluation timelines. Stage 3 – Final Written Warning or Termination: At Stage 3, the employee is placed on a 30-day probationary extension or terminated, at the discretion of the Department Head with mandatory VP approval.

discretion of the Department Head with mandatory VP approval. Termination for cause (e.g., data falsification, harassment, IP theft, biosafety violations) bypasses Stages 1–2 and proceeds directly to Stage 3 or immediate dismissal. All Stage 3 actions require written sign-off from Legal. 1.4.1 Addendum A — Senior Staff PIP Modifications (POL-HR-004-ADDENDUM-A)

A — Senior Staff PIP Modifications (POL-HR-004-ADDENDUM-A) For Tier III and Tier IV employees, the PIP duration at Stage 2 is extended to 90 days (not 60 days as stated in the general policy). The measurable improvement criteria requirement is raised to a minimum of five criteria. A third-party executive coach engagement is mandatory, with costs borne by NexaGen up to $8,000. This addendum supersedes POL-HR-004 Section 2(b) for the duration of any Tier III/IV PIP.

Section 2(b) for the duration of any Tier III/IV PIP. POL-HR-009: Contractor and Consultant Engagement Independent contractors engaged for bioinformatics or laboratory work are compensated at day rates rather than annual salaries. Standard day rates by equivalency tier: Tier I equivalent: $380–$520/day
Tier II equivalent: $550–$780/day
Tier III equivalent: $820–$1,200/day

equivalent: $550–$780/day
Tier III equivalent: $820–$1,200/day All contractor engagements exceeding 90 days or $50,000 in aggregate value require a formal Statement of Work (SOW) reviewed by Legal and countersigned by the relevant VP. Contractors are not eligible for PTO, parental leave, or equity grants. Contractors working on-site must complete the same biosafety orientation as full-time employees (see POL-LAB-002).

biosafety orientation as full-time employees (see POL-LAB-002). SECTION 2: LABORATORY SAFETY AND PROTOCOLS
POL-LAB-001: Biosafety Level Classification and Facility Access
NexaGen operates research facilities across three biosafety levels (BSL): BSL-1 Facilities (Buildings A, B, G): General molecular biology, non-pathogenic E. coli strains, cell culture with non-infectious materials. Access requires completion of Lab Safety Orientation (LSO-101) and a valid NexaGen badge.

of Lab Safety Orientation (LSO-101) and a valid NexaGen badge. BSL-2 Facilities (Buildings C, D, F-West Wing): Work with agents of moderate individual hazard. Access requires LSO-101 plus BSL-2 Certification (LAB-CERT-02), annual refresher training, and a supervisor-countersigned access request (Form LAB-ACCESS-02B). Personnel must log entry and exit in the electronic facility management system (FMS).

and exit in the electronic facility management system (FMS). BSL-3 Facilities (Building E, F-East Wing, and secure suites in Building H): Select agents and toxins, aerosol-transmissible pathogens. Access requires LAB-CERT-02 and LAB-CERT-03, medical clearance from Occupational Health, a two-person rule during all active work sessions, and quarterly re-certification of all personnel. Building H secure suites additionally require Division VP approval and background check on file within the past 24 months.

and background check on file within the past 24 months. Personnel who exit a BSL-2 or BSL-3 facility without completing the exit log procedure are subject to an immediate incident report filed under POL-LAB-008 (Incident Reporting), with mandatory supervisor notification within 2 hours. Three unlogged exits within a rolling 12-month window triggers a Stage 1 disciplinary action under POL-HR-004. POL-LAB-002: Personal Protective Equipment Standards

POL-LAB-002: Personal Protective Equipment Standards PPE requirements vary by facility tier and task type. The following matrix applies: BSL-1 Minimum Standard: Lab coat, nitrile gloves (single layer), safety glasses when handling liquids. BSL-2 Minimum Standard: Lab coat (autoclavable), nitrile gloves (double-layered for cell culture), safety glasses, closed-toe shoes. Face shield required for all centrifuge operations and any liquid volume exceeding 250 mL.

centrifuge operations and any liquid volume exceeding 250 mL. BSL-3 Minimum Standard: Full Tyvek suit or dedicated BSL-3 gowning (donned in anteroom), double nitrile gloves with taped wrists, N95 or higher respiratory protection (PAPR preferred), face shield, dedicated closed-toe footwear that remains in the BSL-3 anteroom. All PPE disposal follows the BSL-3 waste stream (orange-bagged, autoclaved, then incinerated).

waste stream (orange-bagged, autoclaved, then incinerated). PPE procurement is the responsibility of the Laboratory Manager. Researchers may not bring personally purchased PPE for use in BSL-2 or BSL-3 facilities without written approval from the EHS department. The approved PPE vendor list is maintained at the NexaGen intranet portal under EHS > Approved Vendors > PPE (updated quarterly). POL-LAB-005: Chemical Inventory and Hazardous Waste Disposal

POL-LAB-005: Chemical Inventory and Hazardous Waste Disposal All chemicals must be logged in the NexaGen Chemical Inventory Management System (CIMS) within 24 hours of receipt. CIMS is accessible at cheminventory.nexagen.internal. Each chemical entry must include: CAS number, quantity received, storage location (room and cabinet number), SDS document upload, and the name of the responsible researcher.

document upload, and the name of the responsible researcher. Hazardous waste disposal is governed by EPA and state regulations, and NexaGen's contract with ClearWaste Solutions (Contract NXG-EHS-CWS-2022, expiry December 31, 2024). Disposal categories: Category A (Halogenated Solvents): Dedicated red-cap containers in satellite accumulation areas. 55-gallon capacity; containers must be picked up before reaching 90% fill. Pickup scheduled via ClearWaste portal, 5-business-day lead time.

scheduled via ClearWaste portal, 5-business-day lead time. Category B (Non-Halogenated Solvents): Yellow-cap containers. Same capacity and pickup protocol as Category A. Category C (Aqueous Waste with Heavy Metals): White-cap containers. Segregation from Category A/B is mandatory; mixing Category A and C waste constitutes a regulatory violation requiring immediate EHS notification.

a regulatory violation requiring immediate EHS notification. Category D (Biohazardous Waste): Orange-bag collection, autoclave verification stamp required before transfer to main waste stream. See POL-LAB-001 for BSL-3-specific disposal. POL-LAB-008: Incident Reporting and Root Cause Analysis
Any incident, near-miss, or unsafe condition must be reported within 4 hours of observation using the Incident Reporting System (IRS) at safety.nexagen.internal. Incidents are classified as:

(IRS) at safety.nexagen.internal. Incidents are classified as: Level 1 (Near-Miss / No Injury): IRS report required; reviewed by Lab Manager within 5 business days. Level 2 (Minor Injury or Spill < 1L): IRS report required; EHS notification within 2 hours; Occupational Health assessment for any personnel involved.

Occupational Health assessment for any personnel involved. Level 3 (Significant Injury, Spill > 1L, or Regulatory Trigger Event): IRS report required; VP notification within 1 hour; external regulatory agency notification may be required (EHS determines within 4 hours). Facility section may be locked down pending investigation.

Facility section may be locked down pending investigation. Root Cause Analysis (RCA) is mandatory for all Level 2 and Level 3 incidents. RCA must follow the 5-Why methodology and be submitted within 10 business days of the incident. RCA reports are reviewed by the Safety Committee at its monthly meeting (second Tuesday of each month). POL-LAB-014: Authorized Absence from Active Laboratory Experiments

Authorized Absence from Active Laboratory Experiments Researchers engaged in time-sensitive assays (e.g., transfection windows, live-cell imaging, multi-day fermentations) must designate a Coverage Scientist before any planned absence exceeding 2 hours during an active experimental window. The Coverage Scientist designation is recorded in the Lab Notebook Management System (LNMS).

is recorded in the Lab Notebook Management System (LNMS). Unplanned absences during active experiments must be communicated to the Lab Manager within 30 minutes via direct message on the NexaGen internal Slack workspace or by phone; email alone is not acceptable. SECTION 3: DATA GOVERNANCE AND COMPUTATIONAL RESEARCH POLICIES
POL-DATA-001: Research Data Classification Framework
All research data generated at or on behalf of NexaGen is classified into one of four data sensitivity tiers:

NexaGen is classified into one of four data sensitivity tiers: Class 1 – Public: Data approved for external publication or public repository deposition (e.g., GenBank, SRA, GEO). Requires sign-off from the PI and Data Governance Officer (DGO) before deposition. Class 2 – Internal: Preliminary analyses, internal benchmarking results, failed experiment records. Accessible to all NexaGen employees with a valid account. Not for external sharing without DGO approval.

a valid account. Not for external sharing without DGO approval. Class 3 – Confidential: Proprietary assay development data, pre-publication genomics data, clinical trial data under active IND. Accessible only to personnel with project-specific authorization documented in the Project Access Registry (PAR). Requires encryption at rest (AES-256 minimum) and in transit (TLS 1.3).

encryption at rest (AES-256 minimum) and in transit (TLS 1.3). Class 4 – Restricted: Trade secrets, PII associated with human subjects research, regulatory submission dossiers. Accessible only to named individuals with DGO and Legal co-authorization. Stored exclusively on NexaGen-managed servers; no cloud storage permitted without a signed Data Processing Agreement (DPA) reviewed by Legal. Physical access to servers hosting Class 4 data requires two-person authorization.

servers hosting Class 4 data requires two-person authorization. 
POL-DATA-002: Bioinformatics Pipeline Validation Standards
All computational pipelines used to generate data supporting regulatory submissions (IND, BLA, NDA, 510(k)) must undergo formal validation before use. The validation workflow consists of three phases:

before use. The validation workflow consists of three phases: Phase 1 – Analytical Validation: Pipeline is run on reference datasets with known outputs. Concordance thresholds (≥99% for variant calling, ≥95% for taxonomic classification at genus level) must be met and documented. Validation report signed by the lead bioinformatician and the DGO.

report signed by the lead bioinformatician and the DGO. Phase 2 – Operational Qualification (OQ): Pipeline is deployed in the production computational environment (NexaGen HPC Cluster, SLURM scheduler, Singularity containers). All dependencies must be containerized with pinned versions. Pipeline must complete without error on three independent test datasets drawn from the NexaGen Reference Dataset Library (RDL v2.3 or later).

from the NexaGen Reference Dataset Library (RDL v2.3 or later). Phase 3 – Performance Qualification (PQ): Three production runs on actual project data, with output reviewed by an independent scientist not involved in pipeline development. Any discrepancy triggers a deviation report under POL-DATA-007.

Any discrepancy triggers a deviation report under POL-DATA-007. Pipeline version control is mandatory using Git; repositories must be hosted on the NexaGen GitLab instance (gitlab.nexagen.internal). Branches named main and validated are protected; direct pushes are prohibited. All changes to a validated pipeline require a new validation cycle starting from Phase 2 minimum, unless the change is classified as a Minor Modification under Change Control Form CCF-BIO-01.

as a Minor Modification under Change Control Form CCF-BIO-01. POL-DATA-003: Data Storage, Retention, and Backup
Research data retention periods by class: Class 1 data: Minimum 10 years post-publication. Class 2 data: Minimum 5 years from date of creation. Class 3 data: Minimum 15 years, or 5 years post-regulatory approval (whichever is longer). Class 4 data: Minimum 20 years, or as specified in the applicable regulatory framework (ICH E6, 21 CFR Part 11, etc.), whichever is longer.

framework (ICH E6, 21 CFR Part 11, etc.), whichever is longer. All research data must be backed up to the NexaGen primary data center (DC-Primary, San Diego) and replicated to DC-Secondary (Research Triangle Park, NC) within 24 hours. Backup integrity is verified monthly using MD5 checksum comparison. Any backup failure triggers an automated alert to the IT Infrastructure team and must be resolved within 72 hours.

IT Infrastructure team and must be resolved within 72 hours. Storage quota alerts are issued automatically at 75% and 90% of allocated project storage. At 90%, new data writes to the affected directory require DGO approval. Labs consistently exceeding quota thresholds for more than 30 consecutive days are subject to storage reallocation review by the IT Governance Committee.

to storage reallocation review by the IT Governance Committee. Sequencing data (raw FASTQ files) from Illumina, Oxford Nanopore, and MGI platforms are retained at full resolution (no compression) for the duration of the project plus 3 years. Compressed (gzip) copies may substitute after the project closure date if bit-perfect decompression is verified and documented.

date if bit-perfect decompression is verified and documented. POL-DATA-004: Access Control and Authentication
All NexaGen computational systems enforce Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) aligned with the data classification tiers in POL-DATA-001. Specific requirements:

classification tiers in POL-DATA-001. Specific requirements: Multi-factor authentication (MFA): Mandatory for all systems accessing Class 3 or Class 4 data. MFA via hardware token (YubiKey, model 5 series or later) is required for Class 4. Software-based TOTP (e.g., Authy, Google Authenticator) is acceptable for Class 3.

(e.g., Authy, Google Authenticator) is acceptable for Class 3. Password Policy: Minimum 14 characters, complexity requirements (upper, lower, numeric, special). No reuse of the last 12 passwords. 90-day rotation for accounts with access to Class 3 or 4 data; 180-day rotation for Class 1/2.

access to Class 3 or 4 data; 180-day rotation for Class 1/2. Service Accounts: All automated pipeline service accounts must use SSH key pairs (ED25519 or RSA-4096 minimum). Passwords are prohibited for service accounts. Keys must be rotated every 180 days and stored in the NexaGen Vault instance (vault.nexagen.internal).

stored in the NexaGen Vault instance (vault.nexagen.internal). Privileged Access Management (PAM): System administrators accessing production infrastructure use just-in-time (JIT) access provisioning via CyberArk. Session recordings are retained for 12 months. 
POL-DATA-005: Data Breach Notification and Incident Response
A data breach is defined as any confirmed or suspected unauthorized access, disclosure, modification, or destruction of NexaGen research data. Upon suspicion of a breach:

of NexaGen research data. Upon suspicion of a breach: Immediate Actions (0–4 hours): Notify the Information Security team via the security hotline (ext. 5555 or security@nexagen.com) immediately upon suspicion.
Do not attempt to remediate or investigate the breach independently.
Preserve all logs and do not power down affected systems without InfoSec guidance.
Short-Term Response (4–24 hours):

without InfoSec guidance.
Short-Term Response (4–24 hours): InfoSec conducts initial triage. Breach confirmed or unconfirmed determination made within 8 hours.
If confirmed: DGO and Legal notified; severity classification assigned (Class 3 breach = Severity 2; Class 4 breach = Severity 1).
Notification Timelines:

2; Class 4 breach = Severity 1).
Notification Timelines: Class 3 breach (Severity 2): Internal executive notification within 24 hours of confirmation. External regulatory notification (if applicable) within 72 hours per applicable law (GDPR Art. 33, HIPAA Breach Rule, etc.).

per applicable law (GDPR Art. 33, HIPAA Breach Rule, etc.). Class 4 breach (Severity 1): Internal executive notification within 4 hours. External regulatory notification within 72 hours. Affected individuals notified within 30 days. Clinical trial sponsors notified within 24 hours per sponsor agreement. Board-level breach report required for any Severity 1 event. Post-incident review must be completed within 30 days following incident closure.

must be completed within 30 days following incident closure. POL-DATA-006: Data Access Revocation Upon Employee Separation
Upon initiation of an employee's separation (voluntary or involuntary), the following access revocation timeline applies: Immediate (Day 0 – at time of HR notification): VPN access, email, Slack, and remote desktop revoked within 2 hours of HR notification to IT.

remote desktop revoked within 2 hours of HR notification to IT. End of Day 1: All project-specific repository access revoked. Active compute jobs on HPC are suspended (not terminated; data preserved for 14 days for PI retrieval). End of Day 5: All remaining NexaGen system credentials deactivated. Physical access badge deactivated simultaneously.

deactivated. Physical access badge deactivated simultaneously. Day 30: All personally assigned storage directories archived to cold storage. A copy of the access activity log for the departing employee is sent to their former supervisor and retained for 3 years.

is sent to their former supervisor and retained for 3 years. For involuntary terminations-for-cause, Day 0 revocations are expanded to include physical badge deactivation and escort from premises at time of HR notification. No 14-day compute job preservation applies; jobs are terminated and data is immediately archived under DGO supervision.

and data is immediately archived under DGO supervision. 
POL-DATA-007: Deviation and Change Control in Computational Research
Any deviation from a validated pipeline or approved computational protocol must be documented using the Deviation Report Form (DRF-BIO-01) within 24 hours of the deviation occurring or being discovered. Deviations are classified as:

occurring or being discovered. Deviations are classified as: Planned Deviation: Intentional departure from the validated method, approved in advance by the DGO and PI. Requires completion of a risk assessment (Form RA-BIO-01) prior to execution. Unplanned Deviation: Unintended departure, identified during or after execution. Requires immediate notification to PI and DGO. Root cause analysis (see POL-LAB-008 for RCA methodology, also applicable here) must be completed within 10 business days.

applicable here) must be completed within 10 business days. A Planned Deviation exceeding 30 calendar days in duration or affecting more than 20% of pipeline steps automatically triggers a full re-validation per POL-DATA-002.

automatically triggers a full re-validation per POL-DATA-002. Change Control requests for modifications to validated pipelines are submitted via the Change Control Board (CCB) portal at ccb.nexagen.internal. Minor modifications (definition: changes that do not affect algorithmic logic, reference databases, or output schema) may be approved by the DGO alone with a 5-business-day review window. Major modifications require full CCB review (biweekly meeting, second and fourth Monday of each month) plus a new Phase 2/Phase 3 validation cycle.

of each month) plus a new Phase 2/Phase 3 validation cycle. SECTION 4: CROSS-CUTTING POLICIES AND POLICY HIERARCHY
POL-CROSS-001: Policy Conflict Resolution
In the event of an apparent conflict between two NexaGen policies, the following hierarchy governs: Applicable federal and state law (always supersedes internal policy) Regulatory agency guidance (FDA, EPA, OSHA, applicable ICH guidelines) Policy addenda and amendments (e.g., POL-HR-004-ADDENDUM-A supersedes the parent section on its specific topic)

supersedes the parent section on its specific topic) Section-level policies (e.g., POL-DATA-005 supersedes POL-DATA-001 on breach notification specifics) General policies and overarching frameworks When two same-level policies appear to conflict, the more recently dated version governs pending formal resolution by the Office of Compliance. Resolution requests must be submitted via Form NXG-COMPL-01 and are adjudicated within 15 business days.

Form NXG-COMPL-01 and are adjudicated within 15 business days. Required certifications and training completions are tracked in the NexaGen Learning Management System (LMS) at learning.nexagen.internal. Automated reminders are sent at 60 days, 30 days, and 7 days before expiry. Employees with lapsed certifications are automatically locked out of the relevant facility or system access pending renewal. Supervisors receive concurrent notifications. Certifications with cross-policy dependencies:

notifications. Certifications with cross-policy dependencies: Certification	Required by	Validity	Renewal Lead Time LSO-101 (Lab Safety)	POL-LAB-001	3 years	60 days LAB-CERT-02 (BSL-2)	POL-LAB-001	2 years	60 days LAB-CERT-03 (BSL-3)	POL-LAB-001	1 year	90 days Data Governance Awareness	POL-DATA-001	1 year	30 days Biosafety Cabinet Recertification	POL-LAB-002	1 year	30 days GCP Training (clinical staff)	POL-DATA-003	2 years	60 days

days GCP Training (clinical staff)	POL-DATA-003	2 years	60 days Failure to renew LAB-CERT-03 within the 90-day renewal window (i.e., renewal not submitted before the 90-day mark) escalates to a Level 2 disciplinary action, bypassing Stage 1, due to the safety-critical nature of BSL-3 certification lapse. APPENDIX A: GLOSSARY OF TERMS
BSL: Biosafety Level. A system of biocontainment precautions designed to isolate dangerous biological agents.

precautions designed to isolate dangerous biological agents. CCB: Change Control Board. The governance body responsible for reviewing and approving modifications to validated computational workflows. Class [1–4]: Data sensitivity classification under POL-DATA-001. Note: "Class" in this context refers to data classification only; do not confuse with "Tier" which refers to employee classification under POL-HR-001.

which refers to employee classification under POL-HR-001. Coverage Scientist: A designated researcher who assumes responsibility for time-sensitive laboratory tasks during a primary researcher's absence, per POL-LAB-014. DGO: Data Governance Officer. Senior role responsible for oversight of all data classification, access control, and breach response functions. FMS: Facility Management System. Electronic entry/exit logging platform for BSL-2 and BSL-3 facilities.

entry/exit logging platform for BSL-2 and BSL-3 facilities. PAR: Project Access Registry. The authoritative record of personnel authorized to access Class 3 data for specific projects.
